channel.title: Comments for page "Memetics and Infohazards Division Orientation"
channel.description: Posts in the discussion thread "Memetics and Infohazards Division Orientation"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, that one pulled a lot of tri- Oh cool a free book!

item.title: Re: +1
item.content:encoded: Jesus… this thing is loaded with secrets

Holy 173 I love this. I read it forever ago before I knew about all the fuckery and hidden stuff on this site so I was quite surprised with everything. I still feel like im missing something though. there was a link near the end that I only saw for a flash while moving my mouse around

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is probably the trippiest article I have read in a while. Well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Man, the way all the layers of mind-fuckery are written and coded is as impressive as the fiction writing itself. I had to go through it three times to make sure I got everything… and then went ahead and checked the source too, since it suggested it. I love it.

Edit: Noticed the reference to 2357, too. Always did think Dr. Graff was a self-aggrandizing chump taking on people far better at this than she knew…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Now this is cool

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Use of coding is 200% for everyone, and FYI I certainly invented none of it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Can I use the code for the text shenanigans for other tales or SCPs?
or is it plagiarism ?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
THIS. This is cool. It clashes with my headcanon in nice, inspiring ways, and I'd love to know more of Researcher Eksun… if that is even his name.

Upvoted, and may it reach +100 today, because it deserves to be in the 200s.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Correct. Memeticists more than psychics per se, but its an arguably subtle difference depending on how one defines each.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So just to be clear, from a narrative perspective, the idea here is that the Memetics and Infohazards Division is a cover for a secret cabal of psychics within the Foundation ranks who work to recruit psychics to their heterodox project/worldview instead of containing them?

I like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh my lord…
At first I read it and thought it was a cool tale, then somebody on chat pointed out all the hidden stuff. Amazingly trippy and very well-executed, upvoted for sure.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Still one of my favorite tales of 2015. Goddamn fantastic work imo.

item.title: Staff Post - Reversion Notice
item.content:encoded: Version 41 of this article was reverted, as it corrected an intentional misspelling.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I finally listened to this, and I now get how this can work as a story. Upvoting.

item.title: Re: Source code
item.content:encoded:
1. Scroll to the bottom of the page.
  2. Click "+ Options"
  3. Click "Page Source"

item.title: Source code
item.content:encoded: Is there anything hidden in the source code that can't be found just by scrolling/selecting various things in the article? I'm not sure I can properly access website source codes on my PC for some reason.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this orientation. All of the different layers and hidden things to find. it was great.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was very entertaining.

item.title: O>O
item.content:encoded:
Don't mind me random stalker, and not the one's guarding chernobyl either, stopping by to say bravo! Certainly an upvote for you. By the way if you wouldn't mind just slipping me those memetics from the SCP-231-7 that'd be greatttttttttttt. Again I love'd it.

100/1o would read again

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is so cool. I very eagerly await any further updates to the audio version you care to make, and will probably hold off on posting until then.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Since I'm not on Reddit, feel free to post it there.

Also, I can do a re-take with your suggestions to (probably) replace the original.

P.S. I might do a second cut of it, separate from the original, to include and emphasize the parts I didn't include the first time around. Might take a bit longer to make though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow, that is super cool! Hope you like it China!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You sir, are awesome, and those two things in tandem are gloriously weird. Thank you SO much, your adaptation is a treat to listen to.

So, two other things.
1. Would you mind if I posted your adaptation to the SCP subreddit? Or would you want to if you're on the site? People seem to play around with audio a fair amount there, and were supportive of the piece so I think they'd dig it.

2. After listening to it again 4 more times(I'm apparently an egomaniac?) there is just one thing I'd recommend changing. I was surprised the white text in redacted worked as well as it did aurally, but I think merging the last 3 white text parts into their own paragraph instead of leaving them spread out would improve flow.

So it would go as follows: The testing has already begun to see who can pick up all of it. The full spectrum of information all around us is invisible to the sleepwalkers drooling next to you. The number of informational channels you can perceive determines your rank and assignments. But even if you can detect individual phomemes you are still a green as grass rookie
You all carry some form of the XX Gene…

NOTE: I love it as is full stop. I don't think this is by any means necessary or that I mean to dictate how you do it, but I think that would improve the flow of these thoughts on their own, and the transitions between the black text paragraphs, particularly the 'don't worry about your genetic makeup' to 'the other good news'. Let me know what you think and about linking to it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Also I had never heard the Shia LeBouef song and don't know what to make of that either.

Well, that's rather clear what to do next.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This tale got a translation over at the Chinese site! Being on the forums though, their adaptation of the material is certainly different.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
THAT. WAS. FUCKING. AWESOME!

Wow, holy crapy. Holy crap. I love it. A lot. Ha.

As to a retake or my specs I like the effects you'd used and choices you made for the adaptation. I don't really have any meaningful notes. I think the way you structured it made sense as to flow, and not having too many bells or whistles. The 'We' stuff I was more talking about the source code section, WHICH SPEAKS FOR THE COLLECTIVE. Ad infinitum could be sort of glitchy like the gif.

But I like the first two layers though, and now have the strange experience of thinking, man, two more layers on top of what's there? Thats way too much. Quit while you're ahead dude. Also I had never heard the Shia LeBouef song and don't know what to make of that either.

Wow, I am blown away and really pumped to hear it. Very well played sir.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I put it on the audio adaptations page (links to soundcloud)

It's not quite to your specifications, but I'd be happy to do a re-take of it. I'd want to align with the author's intent.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agreed. I loved all the orientation tales, so much I wanted to add my own.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ooh ooh is it online anywheres?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Voice it? But the…and the…what about the other…and then…*sound of brain exploding*

I would absolutely love that, please do. I am very flattered and intrigued by the idea.

In keeping with the spirit of the thing though, I demand one layer be only audible if it is played backwards, another be pitched in a range of sound only audible to bats, and another is in morse code. If listeners aren't willing to put in the effort and use the correct devices, they simply do not deserve access to my beautiful beautiful words.

I kid, I kid. Seriously though, that would be awesome. I don't know how you'd do it, maybe putting in a different tone/range or effect or something for the different layers/extras? You could do multiple people or multiple takes of yourself for the code layer as its phrased as an address from a WE. Whatever you do, I can't wait to hear it though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: we need more orientation articles like this for the different departments.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Actually I already recorded it, and it's a bit different from that.

I emphasized the hidden parts, like someone whispering to you during an assembly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Done, the quotes seemed damned if you do or don't. I put them in because some people were having trouble getting it was meant to be spoken, but that seems to lead to a different sort of confusion. I don't mind making the reader work hard, but don't want that to be due to my poor choices. I think this is a blind spot for me as I've been over it so many times I can't see it with new eyes. Glad you liked it and thanks for the tip!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe a barely-audible overlay for those parts?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How are you going to include the hidden material in audio?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not even The Volgun's readings are "official" readings, so please, feel free.

(I personally like comparing and contrasting The Volgun's reading of the Ethics Committee orientation with GemoDawn's reading. Drastically different feels, and they're both beautiful.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was great. I always love orientation-style tales +1

I know this is usually TheVolgun's thing, but I would LOVE to voice this. Would that be okay?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Liked it, +1. That said, I agree with others that the quotation marks around everything should go. It was confusing from the very beginning as to whether these were supposed to be alternating lines of a conversation, just because of the quotation marks. It might be a turn-off to other less patient people.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can always tone down the shadowing. I'll message you about it so as not to clutter up the thread.

In the meantime, though, here is a copy of the background .gif you already have, except ~16.4MB smaller. The editor GifCam lets you adjust the timespan of individual frames, so you can use one 74.5s frame instead of 298 0.25s frames.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't know it this will help or make a difference, but here's what I was going for. The footnote explains all the deception is part of the training, because memetics override your thoughts, and you need to learn to be vigilant, even from sources you'd think you should be able to trust. The way its done is partly the orientation sarcastic tone, and the other part as misdirection. If you're focusing on what a dick Zack is, then you miss whatever that asshole is really doing.

The drop down was to indicate that the Division recruits ambitious probably worryingly amoral people, and they want to avoid another 2357 or worse. The last bit is a grandiose conception of what the Division thinks is happening in the world. It doesn't mean they're correct, its just their belief structure. The veil meaning keeping SCPs and all the other anomalies secret.

Sorry you found it confusing/didn't dig it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Those images are great and way better than the clean versions I had. Unfortunately the shadowing makes them pop way too much to be background images. When I added them it made it harder to read. I really appreciate you going to the trouble though for a tale you didn't like particularly much.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have to support Kalinin here (btw welcome back!). I didn't get it the first time, since I was reading on my tablet and didn't bother to gloss over anything. I read it the second time, and managed to discover all the hidden text. But even with all that extra stuff, it feels like extra fluff. To me this feels like the guy delivering the orientation is enjoying how much mindfuckery he can do to these poor recruits before saying "surpr1s3!!! :D i have deceived you yet again.

The first one with the redacted was alright. The hidden collapsible had me confused. The footnote made sense, but then you lost me again with the size 0 text.

Maybe I just can't figure it out. But I'm not sure what this supposed to portray beyond putting memes within memes and hiring the select few who aren't phased by said memes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: On the other hand +43 in 48 hours is not to be read as failure…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, thinking on it, I did too heavily lean on the medium being the message with this. I definitely will try to have more specific substance into whatever else I do in this vein rather than the grandiose handwaving allusions. I clearly like format gimmicks, but am leary of just emptying my coding box a bunch of different times on articles as I agree that would get old instantaneously.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I agree with a lot of the comments that perhaps it's not the deepest tale in the world in terms of content, but for me, showmanship is pretty damn big. It's a level of mindfuckery that, on paper, isn't that huge, but the particular way it was done here is a hell of a good show.

I'll upvote this here, but that doesn't mean I'd upvote any more of the same in the future.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As an exercise in using Wikidot formatting in creative ways, this is quite clever. In terms of an actual narrative, it left a great deal to be desired and didn't provide any new perspective beyond the general "Foundation is mysterious, works with weird stuff, and knows everything about anyone ever" baseline.

As others have said, this format is generally an infodump provided courtesy of a researcher who presumably rolls their sleeves up to their elbows and sits backwards in a chair. This article doesn't do anything new with it beyond multiple (mostly irrelevant) format screws and a heaping of mumbo jumbo that would probably fit better at the Wanderers' Library.

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Do that many people really read stuff exclusively on a cellphone? This is an honest question, because it really doesn't strike me as something one needs to go out of their way to accommodate.

This Nielsen study from early 2014 states that people spend an average of 34 hours per month on the internet via smartphone, compared to an average of 27 hours of PC-based internet usage. That proportion is unlikely to have tipped in favor of PCs in the last year and a half.

People don't generally use the internet exclusively from their phones. But the data indicates that most internet usage is mobile now. So yes, we should probably at least consider the mobile experience when we write stuff. People forget a lot of the time that ultimately we're writing for readers. Beyond that, are we really going to argue about the necessity of considering the use of smartphones?

After all, you can't view high resolution images or play high-end games on a cellphone, either, and no one says they should stop making those.

That would make some sort of sense if that was what we did here. Except we don't. We are not purveyors of video games or a clearinghouse of high-resolution imagery. We offer writing. If there is anything that should be accessible from a mobile device, it should be writing. I'm not saying that mobile accessibility should be the only factor to consider when writing a piece, but we're well past the point now where we need to accept that phones are going to be most likely the majority of the devices used to access the site. Authors should not be surprised at downvotes and criticism for failing to take that into account by now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, while I do read from both a laptop and my iPhone, iPhone is more for browsing while I am on the move. If I want to use chat, do draft critiques, post content, the laptop is my weapon of choice.

God, I guess I'm quite an oldie for a young adult.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One of my ideas was a powerpoint style tale, which I think would be technically possible given my abilities, and I may revisit it. A flash puzzle game is a couple lightyears beyond what I'm capable of, but I agree that could be cool.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't have a tablet, and I am unlikely to get one in the foreseeable future. I have an e-ink reader but that is unsuitable for web content for the most part. I therefore do most (90%+) of my casual reading of Web content on my phone. I only generally view this site on my desktop or laptop when I am posting or editing an article. I don't find extended casual reading on a monitor to be generally comfortable and will prefer my Kindle (for books and many PDFs) or my phone (for most web content).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I do a significant amount of my SCP-ing on a phone, yes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Do that many people really read stuff exclusively on a cellphone?
This is an honest question, because it really doesn't strike me as something one needs to go out of their way to accommodate. More of a "when it can be done without being a massive pain, people appreciate it because they can read stuff on lunch break or whenever it is 9-to-5 job people do that stuff" thing.

After all, you can't view high resolution images or play high-end games on a cellphone, either, and no one says they should stop making those.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's why I think a video with low frequency audio tracks, a power point on single frames of a "blank" screen and so on would have worked better for this (although obviously vastly more technically challenging and expensive).

For something textual, I think you might need more clever tricks than mere wiki coding permits and something more like a Flash puzzle game would be what this requires.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wait, was the briefing bees? I didn't mean for it to be bees. It was my first post. Are all first posts bees? Are all the commenters bees? Should I have put more bees in?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My problem is that this is presented as a verbal presentation with hidden hypertext and watermarks. That doesn't seem to work because I can't see how hypertext and source code relate to purely verbal presentation.

This was my initial issue with the draft, but the more I think about it, the more I think that a verbal presentation is the correct answer.

Because if this was written as an introductory email for the memetics department, all of the pop-ups/white text etc would be too easy to take literally (as pop-ups, white text etc). In that case, it's not knowledge only available to the memetic initiate, it's available to anyone who can use a mouse and knows where to look (as per the actual tale).

Or if it was written as an introductory hard copy letter, then the same issues exist - letters don't have pop-ups, collapsibles or a source code. I think the use of written text makes it harder to make the imaginative leap to what the out-of-universe effects represent in-universe.

By making it a verbal presentation, the context is moved so far beyond the out-of-universe form, that I think it actually becomes easier to make the leap to "this is what the initiates are actually hearing when the first layer of words is being spoken". That is hugely helped by the first pop-up or two, which makes this a little clearer.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No its totally fair. I'll try to keep collections of bees in mind more in future works.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hey! I'm really glad to hear you liked how it turned out. And again thanks SO much for all your help and feedback! Yeah, I didn't make any really drastic changes to the overall structure. I tried doing a more serious take but I just couldn't get it to go anywhere, so I tried to work out the kinks instead, and I'm reasonably happy with how the tonal contrasts ended up.

Your vote really means a lot to me. Thanks so much for all the help and support. I'm very much obliged.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Perhaps it is a failure of imagination on my part but I feel like rather than asking me to imagine what a tulip looks like to a bee in the near UV (which I, being bees, can do), you are asking me to imagine what purple tastes like to a turnip. Obviously I am in the minority here, and I certainly appreciate what you've done here, I just can't make it work.
